q: Statements: All pens are books. Some books are tables. Conclusions: I. Some pens are tables. II. Some books are pens.
DESCRIBE: Some books are tables does not guarantee pens-table relation. But all pens are books → some books are pens is valid.

q: Statements: Some cars are bikes. All bikes are buses. Conclusions: I. Some cars are buses. II. All buses are bikes.
DESCRIBE: Some cars → bikes → buses gives some cars are buses. Reverse not valid.

q: Statements: All fruits are apples. All apples are mangoes. Conclusions: I. All fruits are mangoes. II. Some mangoes are fruits.
DESCRIBE: All fruits → apples → mangoes. So both conclusions follow.

q: Statements: Some pens are pencils. No pencil is eraser. Conclusions: I. Some pens are not erasers. II. Some erasers are pens.
DESCRIBE: Pencils and erasers are disjoint → some pens linked to pencils cannot be erasers.

q: Statements: All dogs are cats. Some cats are rats. Conclusions: I. Some dogs are rats. II. Some cats are dogs.
DESCRIBE: Dogs ⊂ cats so some cats are dogs is valid.

q: Statements: Some chairs are tables. All tables are wood. Conclusions: I. Some chairs are wood. II. All wood are tables.
DESCRIBE: Some chairs → tables → wood gives some chairs are wood.

q: Statements: All A are B. No B is C. Conclusions: I. No A is C. II. Some C are A.
DESCRIBE: A ⊂ B and B disjoint C → A disjoint C.

q: Statements: Some mobiles are laptops. Some laptops are tablets. Conclusions: I. Some mobiles are tablets. II. Some laptops are mobiles.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse of 'some mobiles are laptops' is valid.

q: Statements: All pens are ink. Some ink are water. Conclusions: I. Some pens are water. II. Some ink are pens.
DESCRIBE: All pens ⊂ ink → some ink are pens.

q: Statements: No apple is mango. Some mango are banana. Conclusions: I. Some apple are banana. II. Some banana are mango.
DESCRIBE: Only II follows from 'some mango are banana'.

q: Statements: All cats are animals. Some animals are birds. Conclusions: I. Some cats are birds. II. Some animals are cats.
DESCRIBE: Cats ⊂ animals → some animals are cats.

q: Statements: Some A are B. Some B are C. No C is D. Conclusions: I. Some A are not D. II. Some B are not D.
DESCRIBE: B overlaps A and C; C disjoint D → both A and B partially not D.

q: Statements: All dogs are pets. All pets are animals. Conclusions: I. All dogs are animals. II. Some animals are dogs.
DESCRIBE: Transitive relation gives both conclusions.

q: Statements: Some fruits are sweet. Some sweet are sour. Conclusions: I. Some fruits are sour. II. Some sweet are fruits.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse of 'some fruits are sweet' is valid.

q: Statements: No pen is pencil. Some pencils are erasers. Conclusions: I. Some erasers are not pens. II. Some pencils are not pens.
DESCRIBE: Pencil overlaps eraser but disjoint pen → both follow.

q: Statements: All pens are books. Some books are tables. Conclusions: I. Some pens are tables. II. Some books are pens.
DESCRIBE: All pens ⊂ books → reversible conclusion gives some books are pens. No definite link to tables.

q: Statements: Some cars are bikes. All bikes are trains. Conclusions: I. Some cars are trains. II. All trains are bikes.
DESCRIBE: Some cars → bikes → trains gives some cars are trains. Reverse invalid.

q: Statements: All A are B. Some B are C. No C is D. Conclusions: I. Some A are not D. II. Some B are not D.
DESCRIBE: C disjoint D and B overlaps C → both A and B partially outside D.

q: Statements: No pen is pencil. Some pencils are erasers. Conclusions: I. Some erasers are not pens. II. Some pencils are not pens.
DESCRIBE: Pencil overlaps eraser but disjoint pen → both conclusions true.

q: Statements: All dogs are cats. Some cats are rats. Conclusions: I. Some dogs are rats. II. Some cats are dogs.
DESCRIBE: Dogs ⊂ cats → reverse gives some cats are dogs.

q: Statements: Some chairs are tables. All tables are wood. Conclusions: I. Some chairs are wood. II. All wood are tables.
DESCRIBE: Some chairs → tables → wood gives some chairs are wood.

q: Statements: All A are B. No B is C. Conclusions: I. No A is C. II. Some C are A.
DESCRIBE: A ⊂ B and B disjoint C → A disjoint C.

q: Statements: Some mobiles are laptops. Some laptops are tablets. Conclusions: I. Some mobiles are tablets. II. Some laptops are mobiles.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse of 'some mobiles are laptops' follows.

q: Statements: All pens are ink. Some ink are water. Conclusions: I. Some pens are water. II. Some ink are pens.
DESCRIBE: All pens ⊂ ink → some ink are pens.

q: Statements: No apple is mango. Some mango are banana. Conclusions: I. Some apple are banana. II. Some banana are mango.
DESCRIBE: Only second conclusion follows directly.

q: Statements: All cats are animals. Some animals are birds. Conclusions: I. Some cats are birds. II. Some animals are cats.
DESCRIBE: Cats ⊂ animals → some animals are cats.

q: Statements: Some A are B. Some B are C. No C is D. Conclusions: I. Some A are not D. II. Some B are not D.
DESCRIBE: B overlaps A and C; C disjoint D → both conclusions true.

q: Statements: All dogs are pets. All pets are animals. Conclusions: I. All dogs are animals. II. Some animals are dogs.
DESCRIBE: Transitive chain gives both conclusions.

q: Statements: Some fruits are sweet. Some sweet are sour. Conclusions: I. Some fruits are sour. II. Some sweet are fruits.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse relation is valid.

q: Statements: No pen is pencil. Some pencils are erasers. Conclusions: I. Some erasers are not pens. II. Some pencils are not pens.
DESCRIBE: Pencil overlaps eraser but disjoint pen → both follow.

q: Statements: All teachers are educated. Some educated are doctors. Conclusions: I. Some doctors are teachers. II. Some educated are teachers.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse of subset relation holds.

q: Statements: Some A are B. All B are C. Some C are D. Conclusions: I. Some A are D. II. Some B are D.
DESCRIBE: No definite connection from A to D or B to D.

q: Statements: All birds are animals. No animal is plant. Conclusions: I. No bird is plant. II. Some plants are birds.
DESCRIBE: Bird ⊂ animal, animal disjoint plant → I true.

q: Statements: Some students are boys. Some boys are players. Conclusions: I. Some students are players. II. Some boys are students.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse relation valid.

q: Statements: All A are B. Some B are C. No C is D. Conclusions: I. Some A are not D. II. Some B are not D.
DESCRIBE: Standard mixed restriction pattern both conclusions valid.

q: Statements: Some pens are books. All books are papers. Conclusions: I. Some pens are papers. II. Some papers are pens.
DESCRIBE: Pen → book → paper gives both conclusions.

q: Statements: No A is B. Some B are C. All C are D. Conclusions: I. Some D are not A. II. Some B are not A.
DESCRIBE: A disjoint B, B overlaps C → both conclusions true.

q: Statements: Some fruits are apples. Some apples are mangoes. Conclusions: I. Some fruits are mangoes. II. Some apples are fruits.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse relation valid.

q: Statements: All A are B. No B is C. Some C are D. Conclusions: I. Some D are not A. II. Some A are D.
DESCRIBE: A ⊂ B, B disjoint C → A cannot relate to C/D overlap.

q: Statements: Some chairs are tables. All tables are wood. Some wood are metal. Conclusions: I. Some chairs are metal. II. Some wood are chairs.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse subset relation possible.

q: Statements: Some A are B. No B is C. Some C are D. Conclusions: I. Some D are not B. II. Some A may be C.
DESCRIBE: C overlaps D but disjoint B → I definite true, II not possible due to B-C separation.

q: Statements: All A are B. Some B are C. No C is D. Conclusions: I. Some A are not D. II. Some B are not D.
DESCRIBE: A ⊂ B, B partially C, C disjoint D → both A and B partly not D.

q: Statements: Some pens are books. All books are papers. Some papers are ink. Conclusions: I. Some pens are ink. II. Some papers are pens.
DESCRIBE: Only reverse chain from pens→books valid, ink link not definite.

q: Statements: No A is B. Some B are C. All C are D. Conclusions: I. Some D are not A. II. Some B are not A.
DESCRIBE: B overlaps C and C ⊂ D but B disjoint A → both conclusions true.

q: Statements: Some A are B. Some B are C. Some C are D. Conclusions: I. Some A may be D. II. Some B may be D.
DESCRIBE: All are 'some' statements → possibility-based conclusions valid.
